Summary: Systematic elaboration. FOXR2 activation designates a CNS neuroblastoma entity (CNS NB‑FOXR2) consisting of a relatively pure population of CNS‑PNET and enriched for variants that would be classified as CNS neuroblastoma or ganglioneuroblastoma. CNS NB‑FOXR2 tumors show embryonal architecture and small‑cell cytology and frequently contain differentiated areas (neuropil, neurocytic cells, or ganglion cells) and histologic features including perivascular anuclear zones, nuclear palisades, and Homer Wright rosettes. CNS NB‑FOXR2 tumors nearly uniformly express OLIG2 and synaptophysin. In CNS NB‑FOXR2 tumors, FOXR2 activation instigates oncogenic activity. Sequencing identified complex inter‑ and intrachromosomal rearrangements converging on FOXR2 that increased its expression, including fusion transcripts retaining the full FOXR2 coding sequence fused to upstream noncoding exons; tandem duplications on X; a deletion between FOXR2 and MAGEH1; copy‑number changes with an approximately 500 kb upstream deletion potentially fusing to MAGED2; and a mitochondrial insertion in USP51 that created a novel FOXR2 promoter. FOXR2 expression was absent in other CNS tumors and normal brain, consistent with activation via promoters of active genes. One tumor lacking FOXR2 elevation harbored focal MYC amplification and higher MYC expression.

Summary: FOXR2 is essential in neuroblastoma and defines an FOXR2-activated subset that shows an unfavorable clinical outcome. FOXR2 stabilizes MYCN protein and thereby forms an alternative mechanism to MYCN amplification. The mechanism of FOXR2 activation in peripheral neuroblastoma remains elusive. Gene fusions driving FOXR2 expression are rarely detected in peripheral tumors in contrast to CNS neuroblastoma with FOXR2 activation. FOXR2-expressing neuroblastoma showed poor survival across the NB97 and NB2004 cohorts and FOXR2 expression was identified as an independent prognostic factor. The nearly identical 5- and 10-year event-free and progression-free survival in FOXR2-expressing tumors suggests that primarily early events occur in this group. FOXR2-mediated MYCN stabilization may explain the poor clinical outcome and may provide knowledge for targeted treatment of FOXR2-activated neuroblastoma.
Reference: Schmitt-Hoffner, F., van Rijn, S., Toprak, U. H., Mauermann, M., Rosemann, F., Heit-Mondrzyk, A., Hübner, J. M., Camgöz, A., Hartlieb, S., Pfister, S. M., Henrich, K. O., Westermann, F., & Kool, M. (2021). FOXR2 Stabilizes MYCN Protein and Identifies Non-MYCN-Amplified Neuroblastoma Patients With Unfavorable Outcome. J Clin Oncol, 39(29), 3217-3228. https://doi.org/10.1200/jco.20.02540

Summary: Systematic elaboration. FOXR2 activation defines NB-FOXR2, a high-grade tumor of the brain hemispheres, and the tumors map to LHX6+/DLX+ interneuron lineages derived from the medial ganglionic eminence in the developing ventral telencephalon. In mice, prenatal targeting of Foxr2 and p53 loss of function to the ventral telencephalon by in utero electroporation is sufficient to induce brain tumors that recapitulate human NB-FOXR2. These tumors show transcriptional features of ganglionic eminence–derived lineages. Chromatin profiling in murine models showed FOXR2 binds chromatin and associates with ETS transcriptional networks. FOXR2 directly binds transcription factors that coordinate initiation of gliogenesis. These results indicate NB-FOXR2 originates from LHX6+/DLX+ interneuron lineages and suggest FOXR2-induced activation of glial programs may explain the tumors' mixed neuronal and oligodendroglial features. NB-FOXR2 tumors express a transcription-factor fingerprint unique to medial ganglionic eminence–derived neuron lineages and absent in other pediatric brain tumors and in EC-NBs. The absence of this fingerprint in FOXR2+ DIPGs, FOXR2+ EC-NB, and a FOXR2-transduced cell line suggests the fingerprint is maintained from the tumor lineage of origin rather than resulting from FOXR2 activation.
Reference: Jessa, S., De Cola, A., Chandarana, B., McNicholas, M., Hébert, S., Ptack, A., Faury, D., Tsai, J. W., Korshunov, A., Phoenix, T. N., Ellezam, B., Jones, D. T. W., Taylor, M. D., Bandopadhayay, P., Pathania, M., Jabado, N., & Kleinman, C. L. (2025). FOXR2 Targets LHX6+/DLX+ Neural Lineages to Drive Central Nervous System Neuroblastoma. Cancer Res, 85(2), 231-250. https://doi.org/10.1158/0008-5472.can-24-2248

Summary: All passages just state FOXR2-related facts. FOXR2 activation is regarded as pathognomonic for CNS neuroblastoma (NB-FOXR2), but a comprehensive understanding of the landscape for CNS tumors exhibiting FOXR2 activation is lacking. FOXR2 overexpression in embryonal tumors included 11 CNS NB-FOXR2 cases, six pineoblastomas (five PB-FOXR2 and one pineal anlage tumor), and one medulloblastoma. FOXR2 overexpression was not limited to PB-FOXR2 in the pineal region. Except for one SHH‑activated, TP53‑mutant medulloblastoma, the other embryonal tumors with FOXR2 overexpression lacked other pathogenic or likely pathogenic genetic alterations. All CNS NB-FOXR2 were cerebral cortical tumors. Other CNS tumors with FOXR2 overexpression showed regional predilections that depended on tumor type. These results support that CNS tumors with FOXR2 overexpression exhibit diverse histopathologic, molecular, and clinical features.
Reference: Siskar, A. N., Hanzlik, E., Cardenas, M. F., Eldomery, M. K., Pinto, S., Tinkle, C. L., Zhang, Q., Li, X., Lin, T., Dhanda, S. FOXR2 activation is not exclusive of CNS neuroblastoma. Neuro Oncol, 27(7), 1801-1812. https://doi.org/10.1093/neuonc/noaf076

Summary: CNS Neuroblastoma with FOXR2 activation (CNS NB-FOXR2) is one of four tumor groups discovered among previously diagnosed CNS-PNETs.
